topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared glance across the breakroom was a slow, agonizing burn. Sarah stared into her lukewarm coffee, the ceramic mug suddenly feeling heavy and cold in her hands. Mark, across the counter, was fiddling with a sugar packet, his movements jerky. "Wait," Sarah managed, her voice thin, barely above a whisper. "Did you ever go to Camp Whispering Pines?" Mark froze, his hand hovering over the packet. A faint flush crept up his neck. "Uh, yeah. Last summer. It was… memorable." The word hung in the air between them, thick with unspoken history. Sarah felt a tightness grip her chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. She quickly turned away, focusing intently on the chipped paint of the wall behind her, wishing the floor would just swallow her whole.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself looking toward the window, the late afternoon sun casting long, distorted shadows across the carpet. Every time I glanced toward the door, I braced myself. Liam was standing there, his smile wide and utterly disarming. "Hey, Maya," he greeted me, his tone bright, almost too cheerful. My jaw clenched involuntarily. I managed a tight nod, forcing a neutral expression onto my face. "Hi, Liam," I replied, my voice sounding strangely flat. The memory of that week—the sticky bugs, the forced camaraderie—surfaced unbidden, making my palms damp. I retreated to my desk, picking up a pen and deliberately tapping it against the wood, a rapid, uneven rhythm that betrayed the tremor running through my hands.

[story 3]

The email thread had been innocuous enough, a shared anecdote about a terrible campfire story. But when I saw his reply—a specific mention of the lake near the old boathouse—a wave of profound deflation washed over me. I reread the message three times, the words blurring. My stomach twisted into a hard knot, and I had to physically shift in my chair, trying to find a posture that didn't scream distress. When he finally walked by, offering a casual wave, I didn't meet his eyes. I just watched his retreating back, the air suddenly feeling too thin to inhale, and I focused intensely on the pattern of dust motes dancing in the single shaft of light cutting through the office gloom.
